Your accounts were connected just a couple of days ago (June 3), and all five accounts — Ally Online Savings, Chase Total Checking, Microsoft 401(k), Chase Sapphire Preferred, and Robinhood Brokerage — are currently showing $0 balances. This is a data completeness issue rather than a reflection of your actual financial position, so I can't give you a reliable cash assessment based on those figures alone.
That said, your transaction history tells a much more useful story. Here's what I can piece together about your cash situation:
What your transactions reveal
Your regular monthly cash outflows look like this:
Rent: $2,350 (Cascade Apartments, monthly)
Groceries: roughly $650–$700/month (Costco, Trader Joe's, Safeway)
Subscriptions & recurring bills: ~$332/month (Adobe $60, Netflix $23, ClassPass $89, AT&T $72, Lyft $28, Costco Gas $58)
Dining out: ~$160–$200/month
Utilities: ~$89/month (Xfinity)
That puts your estimated core monthly expenses somewhere around $3,600–$3,800/month, excluding irregular items like travel.
Your regular take-home pay from Microsoft appears to be $1,840 biweekly (semi-monthly), which works out to roughly $3,680/month net from your salary alone. In May you also received a $42,000 RSU vest and $1,200 in freelance income — both significant but irregular.
The cash question
A solid emergency fund typically covers 3–6 months of essential expenses. Based on your spending pattern, that would be roughly $11,000–$23,000 in accessible, liquid savings.
Before I can tell you whether you have too much or too little cash, it would really help to know:
What are the actual balances in your Ally savings and Chase checking accounts right now?
Is the $42,000 RSU vest sitting in your Robinhood account, or has it been deployed elsewhere?
Once you share those figures, I can give you a much sharper answer on whether your cash is working hard enough for you — or whether you're holding more than you need.

Results aren't guaranteed. See disclosures.
